title: IO effect semantically overloaded: covers both file I/O and global state access
description: ferrum-lang-ownership.md §2.4 states: 'Any access to mutable state that exists outside the function's own stack frame and is not passed in as a parameter generates ! IO. This includes: process-wide global mutable state, thread-local mutable state.' A function that reads a global counter or a thread-local flag gets the IO effect. But IO conventionally and universally means Input/Output — file operations, network I/O, stdin/stdout. A caller who sees fn get_count(): i32 ! IO will think it reads a file or a socket. This semantic overloading makes effect signatures misleading. Suggested split: ! IO for actual I/O (files, pipes, stdio, sockets), ! Global for global/thread-local mutable state. A function that reads a thread-local counter should be ! Global, not ! IO.
